items.question_text: Under 6-7-1, how is the determination made whether an existing ceiling meets the required circling minimums for a timed approach with only one missed approach procedure?
items.explanation: 6-7-1's note states that MDA (MSL) is compared with ceiling (AGL) plus the airport elevation. This converts the AGL ceiling to MSL for comparison with the MDA.

choices.text: Compare MDA (MSL) with ceiling (AGL) plus the airport elevation.
Compare the reported ceiling directly with the MDA without conversion.
Use only the visibility value; ceiling is irrelevant for missed approach criteria.
Compare MDA (AGL) with the airport elevation minus the ceiling.

items.question_text: Under 6-7-2a, what three elements must the timed-approach clearance include for the succeeding aircraft?
items.explanation: 6-7-2a specifies: (1) clear for approach, (2) descend to the altitude vacated by the preceding aircraft, and (3) leave the FAF/OM/fix-in-lieu inbound at a specified time. Alternatively, when using radar, vector to cross the fix per 6-7-5.

choices.text: Clear for approach only; altitude and time are handled by the tower after handoff.
Clear for approach, descend to the altitude vacated by the preceding aircraft, and leave the final approach fix/outer marker inbound at a specified time.
Clear for approach, maintain present heading, and contact the tower at the final approach fix.
Clear for approach, maintain own separation, and report the field in sight.

items.question_text: Under 6-7-5b (EN ROUTE), what timed-approach wake interval applies to a large aircraft behind a super?
items.explanation: 6-7-5b1(b) requires 3 minutes or 7 miles for a large aircraft behind a super. Compare with heavy behind super (3 min/6 miles) and small behind super (4 min/8 miles).

choices.text: 3 minutes or 7 miles.
3 minutes or 6 miles.
4 minutes or 8 miles.
2 minutes or 5 miles (the default interval).

items.question_text: Under 6-7-5c1 (TERMINAL), what timed-approach wake interval applies to a Category E, F, or G aircraft behind a Category A?
items.explanation: 6-7-5c1(b) requires 3 minutes or 7 miles for Category E, F, or G behind Category A. Category B/C/D behind A use 3 min/6 miles; Category H/I behind A use 4 min/8 miles.

choices.text: 3 minutes or 6 miles (same as Category B, C, or D behind A).
3 minutes or 7 miles.
4 minutes or 8 miles.
2 minutes or 5 miles (the default interval).

items.question_text: Under 6-7-5d, what four factors should be considered when determining whether to increase the interval beyond the published minima?
items.explanation: 6-7-5d lists: (1) relative speeds, (2) existing weather conditions, (3) distance between the approach fix and the airport, and (4) type of approach being made.

choices.text: Relative speeds, existing weather conditions, distance between approach fix and airport, and type of approach.
Aircraft weight category, pilot experience level, runway length, and time of day.
Number of aircraft in the sequence, frequency congestion, tower staffing, and fuel remaining.
Radar availability, DME distance, RVR, and ceiling only.

items.question_text: Under 6-7-7c, when paragraph 6-7-2b is applied and the first aircraft misses an approach, what must the controller do with the second aircraft?
items.explanation: 6-7-7c applies when no alternative missed approach procedure exists and weather is below 6-7-1c requirements. In that case, retain radar control or clear the second aircraft to maintain the last assigned altitude and return to the holding/approach fix to hold.

choices.text: Allow the second aircraft to continue the approach normally since it was already cleared.
Retain radar control or clear the second aircraft to maintain the last assigned altitude and return to the holding/approach fix to hold until traffic conditions permit approach clearances.
Issue a visual approach clearance to the second aircraft regardless of weather.
Cancel the second aircraft's IFR clearance immediately.

items.question_text: Under 6-7-5, what is the correct timed-approach wake interval for a small aircraft behind a super in en route operations?

choices.text: 4 minutes or 8 miles (subpara b1(c)).
3 minutes or 6 miles (same as heavy behind super).
3 minutes or 7 miles (same as large behind super).
2 minutes or 5 miles (the default interval).

items.explanation: 6-7-5B1(c) gives 4 minutes or 8 miles for small behind super. The interval increases with the size difference: heavy 3/6, large 3/7, small 4/8.

items.situation: You are sequencing timed approaches at a terminal facility. A Category H aircraft will follow a Category A aircraft. The standard 2-minute/5-mile interval is proposed.
items.question_text: Under 6-7-5c1(c), what interval must you apply?

choices.text: 4 minutes or 8 miles (Category H or I behind Category A).
2 minutes or 5 miles (the default interval applies).
3 minutes or 6 miles (same as Category H/I behind B/C/D).
3 minutes or 7 miles (same as Category E/F/G behind A).

items.explanation: 6-7-5C1(c) requires the largest terminal wake interval (4 min/8 mi) for Category H or I behind Category A. The default 2-min/5-mi interval is superseded.

items.situation: You applied 6-7-2b (no alternative missed approach, weather below 6-7-1c) and cleared two aircraft for approach — the second only after the first landed. The first aircraft now goes missed while the second is on final.
items.question_text: Under 6-7-7c, what must you do with the second aircraft?

choices.text: Retain radar control or clear the second aircraft to maintain the last assigned altitude and return to the holding/approach fix to hold.
Allow the second aircraft to continue normally because 6-7-2b conditions already handled the separation.
Issue a visual approach clearance to the second aircraft since the first aircraft went missed and the runway is clear.
Do nothing; the tower will handle the second aircraft once it is on the missed approach frequency.

items.explanation: 6-7-7C covers the specific case where 6-7-2b was applied. When the first aircraft goes missed, the controller must retain radar control or clear the second aircraft to hold at the last assigned altitude and return to the holding/approach fix.
